professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been studying how open-source projects sustain themselves this semester. A growing number of maintainers have started charging companies for commercial support or priority bug fixes, even though the code itself stays free to download. Some developers argue this funding keeps critical projects alive and rewards years of unpaid labor, while others worry it quietly splits a community into paying and non-paying tiers and erodes the volunteer spirit. Should maintainers of widely used open-source software charge companies for support? Why or why not?

students.name: Marco
students.text: I think charging companies is justified. Corporations build profitable products on infrastructure they never funded, so asking them to pay for support simply redistributes the burden fairly. Without that revenue, exhausted maintainers abandon projects, and everyone, including hobbyists, loses the software entirely.

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I understand the financial pressure, but introducing paid tiers risks distorting priorities. Maintainers may begin fixing whatever corporate clients demand rather than what the broader community genuinely needs. A donation model or shared foundation preserves independence while still compensating the people doing essential work.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: We've been examining how governments respond to housing shortages this semester. Several regions have begun legalizing accessory dwelling units, the small secondary homes built in existing backyards, to add housing without constructing new towers. Some planners argue this gently increases density and lets ordinary homeowners help ease the shortage, while others contend it strains neighborhood parking and utilities and mostly benefits people who already own property. Should governments actively encourage backyard housing units to address shortages? Why or why not?

students.name: Lena
students.text: I support encouraging them strongly. These units add affordable housing using land and infrastructure that already exist, avoiding the displacement that large developments cause. They also give older homeowners rental income, making the policy economically practical and socially gentle rather than disruptive.

students.name: Jared
students.text: I'm more skeptical, honestly. Backyard units concentrate the new housing in wealthier neighborhoods where families already own spacious lots, so renters with the greatest need rarely benefit. Without complementary investment in transit and utilities, the added density simply overwhelms streets that were never designed for it.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: We've been discussing how friendships form in adulthood this semester. A growing number of apps now match strangers specifically for platonic friendship rather than dating, using shared hobbies and personality quizzes to suggest companions. Some sociologists argue these tools genuinely help isolated adults rebuild social lives that work and relocation eroded, while others believe friendships engineered by algorithms feel transactional and discourage the patient, unplanned bonding that lasting relationships require. Do you think friendship-matching apps are a healthy way for adults to make friends? Why or why not?

students.name: Phoebe
students.text: I see real value in them. Adulthood scatters people across cities and demanding jobs, leaving few natural opportunities to meet compatible strangers. An app that surfaces others seeking connection lowers that barrier, and the friendship itself still develops through ordinary shared experiences afterward.

students.name: Carlos
students.text: I remain unconvinced. Meaningful friendship grows from accumulated shared circumstances, not curated compatibility scores, and treating companions like search results encourages people to discard anyone slightly imperfect. Relying on algorithms may actually deepen the social passivity that left adults lonely in the first place.

professor.name: Dr. Novak
professor.text: We've been studying how communities preserve local memory this semester. A number of towns have started recording oral histories from elderly residents and posting them in publicly searchable online archives. Some historians argue these archives rescue irreplaceable firsthand accounts before they vanish and make local heritage accessible to everyone, while others worry that publishing private recollections online exposes vulnerable people and strips stories of the context a careful interview would preserve. Should communities publish residents' oral histories in open online archives? Why or why not?

students.name: Wendy
students.text: I firmly support open archives. Ordinary residents witness historical shifts that official records ignore entirely, and once these elders pass away that knowledge disappears forever. Making the recordings freely searchable ensures future generations and researchers can actually reconstruct how communities genuinely experienced change.

students.name: Tim
students.text: I'd urge more caution here. Personal memories often implicate neighbors or reveal sensitive family details that narrators never imagined broadcasting globally. Communities should secure informed consent and perhaps restrict access, because preserving heritage shouldn't come at the cost of an elderly person's dignity or privacy.
